blocks.text: Important: your child does not need to be with you until the videos begin.
blocks.title: Overview of 'Who will do the puzzle?'

blocks.text: From start to finish, today's activity will take about 20 minutes. First we have some questions for you (the parent) to answer. Your child does not need to be with you for this part.

Then we'll ask you to consent to video recording with your child present. Your child will hear about some children doing activities, and we will ask whether they think the children's parents will help them.

There are no right or wrong answers to the questions we’re asking – we just want to know what your child thinks, so we kindly ask that you let your child respond freely to the questions. For the duration of the study, please sit behind your child or out of your child's eyesight to avoid influencing their answers. However, if your child needs help clicking their answer choices, please help them do so.

Please click the next button to consent to answering the survey questions. After the survey, you will be prompted to consent to video recording with your child present, and then we will start your child's portion of the study.

parent-consent.summary_statement: We are asking you to join a research study. Observing your behavior during this experimental session will help us to understand how adults think about the social world. Study procedures include simple activities, like listening to stories, playing games, and watching videos. If you are interested in learning more about the study, please continue reading, or have someone read to you, the rest of this document before you make your decision. Ask study personnel questions about anything you do not understand. Once you understand the study, please follow the instructions on the right-hand side of the screen to complete the consent process.
parent-consent.datause: All of your responses will be held confidential, and all data will be stored on a secure, password- protected Yale drive. Only the researchers involved in this study and those responsible for research oversight (such as representatives of the Yale University Human Research Protection Program, Yale Institutional Review Boards, and others) will have access to any information that could identify you that you provide. We will share it with others if you agree to it or when we have to do it because U.S. or State law requires it. For example, we will tell somebody if we learn that you are hurting a child or an older person. When we publish the results of the research or talk about it in conferences, we will not use your name. We will not ask you for any additional permission.
parent-consent.use of data by Lookit: The Lookit core team at MIT will also have access to the data collected during this session, in addition to your account data. They use this data to run and improve Lookit, for instance to provide technical support, check how well data collection is working, or see whether Lookit is reaching a diverse group of families. Lookit stores data indefinitely unless you withdraw your recordings at the end of the study.
parent-consent.payment: There are no known risks associated with this study. Although this study will not benefit you personally, we hope it will add to our understanding of how adults think about the world. We cannot and do not guarantee or promise that you will receive any benefits from this study, apart from the satisfaction of participating in cognitive development research.
parent-consent.purpose: We are conducting a research study to examine how adults learn and think about the social world.
parent-consent.procedures: Participation in this study involves completing a questionnaire about your attitudes and beliefs about the social world or simple scenes involving people and groups. These questions are straightforward, and they will either be read to you or you will read them on a computer. These questions do not have a correct answer. Our only purpose is to understand how adults think. We anticipate that your involvement will require less than 5 minutes.
parent-consent.participation: You are free to choose whether to be in this study. If you do choose to participate, it's okay to stop at any point during the session.
parent-consent.publication of results: The results of this study may be presented at scientific meetings or published in scientific journals. We may publish individual responses that cannot identify people, like looking times or sequences of button presses. We never publish birthdates or names. Even if you choose to share your recordings, we never publish information that would make it possible to link recordings with your demographic data.
parent-consent.data collection and webcam recording: During the session, you will be recorded by your computer's webcam and microphone. These webcam recordings are sent securely to the Lookit platform, and other data like answers you enter in forms are sent to the Qualtrics platform. You can view your past webcam recordings on Lookit at any time. Data are stored securely on Lookit and Qualtrics servers and by researchers, and are only shared as described in this document. However, there is always a small risk that data transmitted over the internet may be intercepted or that the security of stored data may be compromised. We will first check that you really agreed to participate by watching your consent recording. If we cannot confirm that you agreed to participate, no one will view any other recordings from this session. At the end of the session, you will choose a privacy level for your webcam recordings. You will also have the option to withdraw your recordings. If you do, only your consent recording will be kept.
parent-consent.research_rights_statement: You are not waiving any legal claims, rights or remedies because of your participation in this research study. If you are not satisfied with how this study is being conducted, or if you have any concerns, complaints, or general questions about the research or your rights as a participant, please contact the Yale Human Research Protection Program to speak to someone independent of the research team at 203-785-4688. You can also write to the Yale Human Research Protection Program, Yale University, HRPP@yale.edu.

schema.title: Parent Survey: Please rate how likely you would be to step in and complete each of the following tasks for your child.

puzzle.title: Imagine your child is struggling to solve a puzzle. How likely would you be to step in and complete this task for your child?

math.title: Imagine your child is struggling to count out marbles and put them next to the numbers 1-6 (e.g., put four marbles next to the number 4). How likely would you be to step in and complete this task for your child?

buttons.title: Imagine your child is struggling to button their sweater. How likely would you be to step in and complete this task for your child?

sandwich.title: Imagine your child is struggling to make a sandwich. How likely would you be to step in and complete this task for your child?

schema.title: Parent Survey: How capable do you think your child is at each of the following tasks?

math-capable.title: Counting out marbles and putting them next to the numbers 1-6 (e.g., putting four marbles next to the number 4)

schema.title: Parent Survey: How much do you think your child can learn from doing each of the following tasks independently?

math-learn.title: Counting out marbles and putting them next to the numbers 1-6 (e.g., putting four marbles next to the number 4)

schema.title: Parent Survey: Now please think about the skills your child can learn from doing these tasks independently. How important is it to you that your child learns these skills?

math-learn-important.title: Counting out marbles and putting them next to the numbers 1-6 (e.g., putting four marbles next to the number 4)

video-consent.summary_statement: We are asking your child to join a research study. The purpose of this study is to examine how children think and learn. Study procedures include simple activities, like listening to stories, playing games, and watching videos. The study takes place within one 20-minute session. There are no known risks associated with this study, and there are no direct benefits to your child. Taking part in this study is your and your child’s choice. You can choose for your child to participate or not participate, and you can change your mind at any time. Your child may assent to participate or not participate and change their mind at any time. Whatever choice you make, you and your child will not lose any legal rights or benefits. If you are interested in learning more about the study, please continue reading, or have someone read to you, the rest of this document before you make your decision. Ask study personnel questions about anything you do not understand. Once you understand the study, we will ask you if you wish for your child to participate; if so, you will complete this consent.
video-consent.datause: All of your child’s responses will be held confidential, and all data will be stored on a secure, password-protected Yale drive. Only the researchers involved in this study and those responsible for research oversight (such as representatives of the Yale University Human Research Protection Program, Yale Institutional Review Boards, and others) will have access to any information that could identify you that you provide. We will share it with others if you agree to it or when we have to do it because U.S. or State law requires it. For example, we will tell somebody if we learn that you are hurting a child or an older person. When we publish the results of the research or talk about it in conferences, we will not use your child’s name. We will not ask you for any additional permission.
video-consent.use of data by Lookit: Because this study is being run on the Lookit platform, researchers working on the Lookit project at MIT will also have access to the data collected during this session, in addition to your account data, child profiles, and demographic survey responses. This data may be used by Lookit to detect and fix technical problems or identify new features that would be helpful; provide support to study researchers; assess data quality (for instance, how well an observer can tell which direction children are looking); evaluate the site's success in reachinsg a diverse population; and characterize family engagement (for instance, looking at what aspects of a study make parents more interested in coming back later).
video-consent.payment: There are no known risks associated with this study. Although this study will not benefit your child personally, we hope it will add to our understanding of how children learn about the world. If your child says that they does not want to continue participating for any reason, you may end the session immediately with no penalty. We cannot and do not guarantee or promise that you will receive any benefits from this study, apart from the satisfaction of participating in cognitive development research. You will, however, receive $5 to thank you for your participation.
video-consent.purpose: We are conducting a research study to examine how children learn and think.
video-consent.procedures: Participation in this study will involve a simple activity such as listening to a story about people, playing with a computerized toy or game, or watching a video. For example, your child may be asked to make judgments about characters they learned about, such as “Who is nice?” or “Who tried harder?” or asked which toy they want to play with and why. These questions are straightforward, and they will either be read to your child, or your child will read them on a computer. These questions do not have a correct answer. Our only purpose is to uncover how children think and learn. We anticipate that your child’s involvement will require less than 30 minutes.
video-consent.participation: Participation in this study is completely voluntary. You and your child are free to decline to participate, to end participation at any time for any reason, or to refuse to answer any individual question without penalty or loss of compensation.
video-consent.publication of results: The results of the research may be presented at scientific meetings or published in scientific journals. Raw data (for instance, looking times to the left and right of the screen) may be published when it cannot identify children. We never publish children's birthdates or names, and we never publish your demographic data in conjunction with your child's video.
video-consent.data collection and webcam recording: During the session, you and your child will be recorded via your computer's webcam and microphone. Video recordings and other data you enter are sent securely to the Lookit platform and stored indefinitely. At the end of the session, you will be prompted to choose a privacy level for your webcam recordings. You will have the option to withdraw your video data at this point. You can view your past recordings on Lookit under 'Studies' -> 'Your past studies' at any time. Data is stored securely on Lookit servers and by researchers. However, there is always a small risk that data transmitted over the internet may be intercepted or that the security of stored data may be compromised. No video clips will be published or shared unless you allow this when selecting a privacy level. If we do not receive a consent recording (the video you'll make to the right) for this session and cannot verify that you agreed to participate, no other video from your session will be viewed. Your child’s name will not be attached to the video and/or audio. The video and/or audio will be stored in a Yale-managed storage system which is only accessible to our researchers. These files are used for coding your child’s responses and ensuring our researchers conducted the studies properly.
video-consent.research_rights_statement: You are not waiving any legal claims, rights or remedies because of your participation in this research study. If you are not satisfied with how this study is being conducted, or if you have any concerns, complaints, or general questions about the research or your rights as a participant, please contact the Yale Institutional Review Board (IRB) to speak to someone independent of the research team at 203-785-4688. You can also write to the Yale IRB, Yale University, humansubjects@yale.edu.

blocks.text: During the activity, we ask that you sit behind your child.

This is because your child is learning from you all the time, and may pick up on even very small cues about what you think. But if your child can't see you, we're definitely only measuring your child's own beliefs.

If you'd like to see the pictures your child will be shown, you can take a look ahead of time now. It's important that you preview these without your child, so that the pictures will still be new to them.

stimuli.caption: Your child will see pictures of a character doing an activity, like this puzzle.
Your child will also see the character's parent, and we will ask your child whether they think the parent will help the child do the activity.
Your child will click on the answer they think is right, and we will ask them to say why they chose that answer.

video-preview.previewButtonText: I'd like to preview the materials

blocks.text: Now please make sure your child is with you. We will ask you to consent to video recording with your child present, and then we will start your child's portion of the study.
Again, the study will take about 20 minutes to complete. After that, you will be able to select a level of privacy for your data.

listblocks.text: Your child will hear stories about children doing activities like making a sandwich or buttoning a sweater, and we will ask your child whether they think the parent in the story helped the child do the activity.
Your child will click on the answer they think is right, and we will ask them to say why they chose that answer.

If your child does not answer out loud, you can prompt them by asking why they chose that answer, but please do not suggest an answer or tell them they are right or wrong. We just want to know what your child thinks!
If your child gets distracted, or you need to attend to something else for a moment, you can pause the study by pressing the space bar.

If you need to end the study early, try closing the window or tab and you should see an 'exit' option pop up. You’ll be prompted to note any technical problems you might be experiencing and to select a privacy level for your videos.

blocks.text: Please turn the volume up so it's easy to hear but still comfortable.
blocks.title: Test your audio

mediaBlock.warningText: Please try playing the sample audio.

listblocks.text: Please make sure that your child is visible in the webcam.
You can say hi to the camera!

Please sit behind your child and allow your child to answer the questions on their own. Please don’t correct your child if you disagree, or tell them they’re right if you agree. We’re just interested in what they think! If your child isn’t sure or doesn’t want to answer a question, you can encourage them to make their best guess.
However, if your child is having any issues clicking the images, you may help them click.

In this activity, your child heard about characters doing some activities. We asked whether your child thought each character's parent would step in and complete a task for their child. We also asked you (the parent or legal guardian) about whether you would step in and complete the same tasks for your child if they were struggling. In these questions there were no right or wrong answers! Broadly, we are interested in whether children expect parents to step in (e.g., because they know parents are kind and helpful) or step back (e.g., because they know parents want their children to learn to do the tasks on their own). We are also curious about whether children's predictions about the characters in the study will relate to what their own parent reported here. For example, does your child expect a character's parent to finish a puzzle if you (the parent or legal guardian) indicated that you would help your child finish a puzzle?

Critically, as mentioned above, there were no right or wrong answers to the questions in the parent survey or in the child-directed portion of the study. We are so grateful for your participation, which will help us learn a great deal more about how children think about the role of parents in their success. You can find more of our lab's work at on our website. If you'd like to learn more about how children understand parenting, check out this article! If you'd like to learn more about overparenting, check out this article!

blocks.title: Your $5 gift card will arrive within 7 days.
blocks.text: Please contact learninglab@yale.edu if you have any issues accessing your gift card.

frameList.selectNextFrame: function(frames, frameIndex, frameData, expData, sequence, child, pastSessions) { { return frameIndex + 1; } }
function(frames, frameIndex, frameData, expData, sequence, child, pastSessions) { if (frameData.selectedImage == '5') { return frameIndex + 45; } else { return frameIndex + 1; } }
function(frames, frameIndex, frameData, expData, sequence, child, pastSessions) { if (frameData.selectedImage == 'practice2_swings') { return frameIndex + 2; } else { return frameIndex + 1; } }
function(frames, frameIndex, frameData, expData, sequence, child, pastSessions) { { return frameIndex + 2; } }

function(frames, frameIndex, frameData, expData, sequence, child, pastSessions) {  { return frameIndex + 2; } }

function(frames, frameIndex, frameData, expData, sequence, child, pastSessions) {  { return frameIndex + 2; } }
function(frames, frameIndex, frameData, expData, sequence, child, pastSessions) { if (frameData.selectedImage == '3-choice2') { return frameIndex + 2; } else { return frameIndex + 1; } }
function(frames, frameIndex, frameData, expData, sequence, child, pastSessions) {  { return frameIndex + 2; } }
function(frames, frameIndex, frameData, expData, sequence, child, pastSessions) { if (frameData.selectedImage == '4-choice2') { return frameIndex + 2; } else { return frameIndex + 1; } }
function(frames, frameIndex, frameData, expData, sequence, child, pastSessions) {  { return frameIndex + 2; } }
function(frames, frameIndex, frameData, expData, sequence, child, pastSessions) { if (frameData.selectedImage == '5-choice2') { return frameIndex + 2; } else { return frameIndex + 1; } }
function(frames, frameIndex, frameData, expData, sequence, child, pastSessions) {  { return frameIndex + 2; } }
